CURRENT AFFAIRS,

"The endeavour to keep Irish work in Ireland is in a
sense everyone’s business: it is tl}e §pecial and parti-
cular concern of those whose material interests are iden-
tical with any branch of work. We would like to see
the various trade socicties and clubs directing their at-
tention to vigorously fighting their own l)m‘txcular
corners. Everyone would like, so far as possible, that
all Irish printing should be done in Ireland, and we are
glad to note that the Dublin Typographical Society, the

~ members of which are personally intercstedr have stirred
themselves. They have formed a comnuj,tee for the
purposeof helping the unemployed of their own trade
or the purpose of endeavouring to keep all Jocal print-
g in the city. For our part so long as Trish printing
done in Treland we are not concerned whether it is
in Dublin, Cork, Limerick, or anywhere else, but
o Dublin printers very reasonably have a keon concern
their own members. We would wish
ades. society and branch of a trade

society would form a committee with a like object. .The
Irish public during the last five years have, all things
considered, done a lot for Irish manufacture, b'ut they
cannot do everything, and those who stand to gain, whe-
ther they be manufacturers, distributors, or artisans,
are neglecting an opportunity which was created for
them if they do not in their individual and corporate
capacities throw themselves into the movement. Many
of the manufacturers have scen and are seeing the need
of advertising in the LEapEr, the organ and pioneer of
the Industrial Revival, and of rallying to the side of
the various industrial organisations; and we are glad
that the Dublin printers have now formed a committee
to look after a matter which so individually concerns
themselves.

Might we point out to the Dublin printers the little
fact that an advertisement bill of the Pantomime now
going on at the Gaiety is before us. If we may claim
to have any eye for a neat job in this line, we cannot
say that we admire this production. The imprint on
this Gaiety Theatre bill is that of a firm in Leeds! A
correspondent makes the following caustic suggestion—
“This little bill has just been handed to me in the
street, and has caused a brilliant idea to take posses-
sion of my whole being. It is this. There is a large
number of Irishmen connected with the Printing Trade
who are now walking the streets of Dublin through lack
of employment. Could not your paper organise a grand
treat for their children—as the parents cannot afford
it—and enable these poor innocents to go and enjoy
the delights of this imported Pantomime. The children
need not be told where the Gaiety people have their
printing done.”

The latest “dump’’ over here is Walter Long, the

eminent dog muzzler.  Having been kicked ignomini-
ously out of Bristol, a crowd of anti-Irish non-descripts
are seeking to “ dump ’’ him into South Dublin. We
have read the names of his various proposers, seconders
and assentors in Lord Ardilaun’s Buff. Most of the
names are mere names, and no more to us; some of them
have been heard of before outside their respective
parishes. In the first paper the eminent dog muzzler
is proposed by Mr.  Pursey Burnard,”’” who, no doubt,
out of gratitude for favours to come, stepped down to
make room for the rejected of Bristol. The “dump’’
that Bristol branded as stale goods is seconded in the
first paper by whom do you think? By our friend, the
tame “Idolator,”” Charles E. Martin, D.L. Amongst
the assentors on the first paper is that world-famous
“Irish ”” poet, Professor Edward Dowden!

On the second paper the proposer is our old friend,
Brother Goulding, of the Great Sick and Sourface Rail-
way, who never had the manhood to answer the straight
question whether or not he agreed to give his whole
time to the manure business; the seconder is Sir Mau-

‘rice Dockrell, who was recently on the stump in Eng-

land in the anti-Irish interest; one of the assentors is
Charles L. Matheson, K.C., the one-time rejected of St.
Stephen’s Green Division.  An assentor on the third
paper is one Arthur Warren Samuels, K.C. We have
a hazy recollection that he was politely declined by some
constibuency also. The seconder on the fourth paper
is one Hon. Arthur de Montmorency. Now, who in the
world can he be?! He hardly comes from Ballyvourney.
One of l;hel assentors on this paper is no other than a
person familiar to our readers, Captain Wade Thomp-
son, the Pervert Priests’ Protector! Another is James
H. North, the auctioneer, who is anxious to dump ”’
into South Dublin the dog muzzler who was kicked out
of Bristol.

On the seventh paper the ex-chief secretary to the
Orangemen is proposed by one Martin Bodkin ! We

wonder who is he? An assentor is one Clifford Lloyd.
Clifford Lloyd! We think we have heard that name
before. One of the assentors on the eighth paper is a
Richard Thomas Hearn, M.D. This Dr. Hearn is the
medical officer of Rathmines district, where the rather
exclusive Commissioners have managed to keep paid ap-
pointments almost exclusively in the hands of those
who, like Dr. Hearn—who, by the way, was branded in
Trinity—dig with the proper foot. Perhaps Long
would get a Bristol dispensary for Hearn—if the former
gets in. The seconder on the tenth paper is Mr. Wil-
Ham P. Geoghegan—we presume he is the Geoghegan
who is one of the eminent directors of the eminently
unsatisfactory Great Sick and Sourface Railway. The
proposer on the sixteenth paper is one Alfred Manning.
We presume this is the shopkeeper in Grafton Street
who is connected with the very-much-proposed “ Inter-
national ”’ Exhibition. If South Dublin does not eat
Bristol refuse after these names, West Britain, South of
v the Boyne, had better put up its shutters.

The anti-Irish candidate for Stephen’s Green issued
posters inscribed ‘‘ Vote for Ireland.”’ The majority
of the electors did so, and, therefore, Mr. I-ah-land
can still devote his whole time to the off-license business.

The Freeman had much to say about the Sligo ““ God
Save the King” incident; also about the storming of
the organ at the recent conferring at the Royal Univer-
sity. We do not see that it has any remarks on the
toasting of “ the King *’ at the annual dinner in Dublin
of the Irish branch of the British Institute of Journal-
ists. AIf Foz tells us that “ The toast of the King
having been honoured,” Tony Traill, the well-known
bigot of the stale-herring university, made a speech.
Where were the Freeman staff, and why did they not
protest against that toast, which in this country, it is
generally admitted, signifies “ Down with the ‘ idolators’
and Nationalists*’? TIf they do mot see their way to
protest against this toast on their own ground why not
3 be honest and stand up for the honouring of it else-
o where? The musical side of the function was largely

contributed to by artistes of the imported operatic stage.
One of these sang the stage Irish travesty on the Irish
clergy, “ Father O’Flynn,”’ and, needless to say, there
was not even one song in Irish. Oh, what a press!

Mr. George Crosbie, of the ““ only National ”’ of Cork,
played “the eloquent Dempsey '’ on the industrial
strings. But why did he not protest against the toast
of “the King’’? Why did he not use his influence to
have even one song in Irish during the evening.  The
*only National ”’ man said:— From some points of
view he thought the Irish people had less patriotism
than any people on the face of the earth.”” We quite
agree. Of course the eloquent Mr. Crosbie was at the

= time playing the cheap patriot on the Industrial ticket.
Might we say that if the Irish people had not less
patriotism in some respects than any people on the face
of the earth the poor old “only National ’’ would get
short, shrift.

There is plenty of war-paint about our mild friend,
the Belfast News Letter, of January 17th. Its second
leading article opens this way:—

“No traffic with traitors, no tampering with the Union; one
Empire, one King, ono Parliament.” These words, with which
Colonel Wallace closed his speeoh at the great Orange demonstra-
tion in the Ulster Hall, last night, oxpress the unalterable doter-
mination of all true Irish Unionists. The Orange Institution has
ever been foremost in defence of the Union and of Protestantism,

_ and it is right that it should make its power felt at this grave
orisis in the history of our country.

Not much of the spirit of Tone or Davis about that, eh?

Self-revelation on the part of anyone is interesting
1o the student of affairs. We would like to put the
X-rays on a Belfast bigot, but one of them has written
y letter which is published in a British paper that is as

 luminous as if the bigot’s mind and heart were laid bare
: er a sort of X-rays. A letter from the Rev. Alex.

5 @‘ék;o' Ashworth, who styles himself “ Minister York

THE LEADER.

JANUARY 27, 1906.

Street Non-Subs. Presbyterian Church, Belfast,”” ap-
pears in the Pontypool I'ree Press of January 19th.
The letter is introduced to the free a..nd independent
readers of the Pontypool Free Press in this way:—
« Mr. F. W. Harding, The Elms, Pqnbypool Road, has
requested us to publish the following letter from a
friend of his in Belefast.”” The Rev. Alex. Osborne Ash-
worth states that he is “an Englishman resident now
for over fourteen years in Ireland.”’ In the course of
this “ Christian *’ minister’s letter we read :—

«,_ . . . for that (Home Rule) means interminablo anarchy,
and bloodshed in the streets of our cities. It means the closing
of our warchouses, the stopping of our manufactorics, nn:l the
conversion of our busy thoroughfares into grass-grown lanes.'

Here is another quotation from the letter of this “Chris-
tian ’ minister and  intelligent ”” Englishman : —

The intelligent Irishman hates the very mention of Home Rule

‘as a possibility, and it is to be hoped the people will not be

hoodwinked by tho manauvres of the coming party who in their
specchos profess that Fomo Rulo is not in their programme, but
aro willing to take any instalments of legislation in that direction.
As far as wo in Bolfast aro concerned, I know there is no un-
certain voice. Divided socially and religiously wo are, I fear,
all too much—oven the Protestants; we aro united on ono thing,
at any rate, and in tho memorable words of the Duke of Abercorn
at tho great Unionist convention fourteen years ago, “We will
not have Home Rulo” hecause we know it will sacrifico our
homes and hand over our agricultural and commercial interests
to tho mismanagement of a set of demagogues stirred chiefly by
solfish interosts and unrighteous ambitions, and whoso incapacity
and intornal quarrels have become a byword amongst all who
Jnow them and take account of their doings. If wo admit these
men to power wao botide us! The curse of God will surely follow
us if wo aro slack in thought or act in such a eupremo crisis. The
things wo hold most sacred will be lost if we do not do our duty
now—freedom to think, liberty to labour, opportunity to foster
education and_the refining virtues—the land will ba laid waste,
tho already disproportionate number of unemployed will be in-
definitely increased, and capital, having no remunerative invest-
ment hore, will leave our shores.

Now that pious English minister ought to go back to
England—say, to the PotFeries!

The “ Grand Master ”” of the “ Independent ’’ Orange-
men is finding out that there is organised tyranny even
amongst non-“ Idolatrous ’’ circles in this country. We
take the following from his sectarian paper: —

Until we came into contact with theso official Unionist organisa-
tions wo confined ourselves almost exclusively to the exposure
and denunciation of the tyranny of tho priest in_politics. We
have beon forced to recogniso tho fact that organised tyranny is
not to be found exclusively in Ultramontane ranks, There is in
Ulster to-day an_organised tyranny—posing as Protestant and
Unionist—which differs in no material sense from the religious
and political tyrannies which Unionists dread most under Home
Rule. This is no cxaggerated assertion, but a painful and self-
ovident fact. Protostant Ulster is in bondage, and individuality
is no longer a Protestant virtue, but a political heresy which is
boing stamped out under the iron wheels of the Ulster Unionist
juggernaut.

Here is a pretty picture culled from the same paper
as to how “Civic Virtue”’ trickles over the “loyal”
parts of Ulster:—

Sitting in an obsoure lawyer's officc in Belfast, ono of the
Carlton Club agents pulled for years the caucus wires all over
Ulster, and selected candidates in every constituency without con-
sulting tho elactors. Carlton Club gold was spent freely, and
ovory hireling had his price. Jobbery and corruption and a
high-handed contempt for the constitutional rights of tho electors
havo beon the outstanding characteristics of Ulstor roprosentation
for the past contury. Lord Atkinson is the latest Gevernment
hack to receive his price.

The appearance of Z'ke Irish Theological Quarterly is,
in its own way, a sign of the general revival of thought
in Ireland. Ireland has witnessed an undoubted
thought revival within recent years, and a natural, if
not an inevitable, outcome of that thought is a keener
interest in the deeper reaches of thought—in philo-
sophy and in theology. A mere revival of what we
may class as popular thought, were it possible, would
be a lop-sided business. A~ thought revival to be
wholesome must be a revival on all sides. Weo welcome
the appearance of T'he Irish Theological Quarterly, which
is published by Messrs. M. H. Gill and Son, and may
be purchased at 2s. per copy, or for 7s. 6d. for the four
quarterly copies. The editors are Rev. Walter
McDonald, D.D.; Rev. Joseph MacRory, D.D.; Rev.

John M. Harty, D.D.; Rev. Patrick J. Toner, D.D., =

(Goodbody

JANUARY 27, 1906.

and Rev. Jameg MacCaﬁrey, S.T.L., Professors in the

. Faculty of Theology at Maynooth. Dr.M‘Donald contri-

butes two articles, the first— Some Tendencies of Mo-
dern Apologetics: Proof of Theism ”’—will be found in-
teresting, if somewhat stubborn, reading to the intelli-
gent layman ; his other article—* About Socialism *’—is
more perhaps to the layman’s taste. A very readable
and interesting article for the goneral reader is that on
“ The Church and the Biblical Question,” by Rev. Dr
MacRory. We have read these three articles, and the
other three articles we intend to read; until we have
read the latter three, we cannot, of course, say any-
thing about them. The Quarterly also contains notes and
book reviews. We trust that not only the clergy, but
the educated Catholic laity, will, in large numbers, sub-
scribe to this Maynooth Quarterly. It adds a cubic to
the volume of national self-esteem to witness the es-
tablishment of such a periodical in the arena of higher
thought. Ireland badly wanted it, and we hope now
that she has got it she will adequately support it.

We thought that there was a law in existence forbid-
ding the granting of licences to new bungeries within a
certain limit of time not yet expired. Be that as it may
the village of Broadford, in Limerick is now blessed with
a “ new licensed establishment and bonded warehouse ’’
—at least we are sure that that is what it will be called
—in addition to the number recently added to the
“commerce and industry ’’ of that hive of industry.
That Broadford is a hive of industry may be gathered
from the fact that it is an Irish village with a popula-
tion of 120 souls. The burning thirst of this populous
district a while ago was inadequately ministered to by
stz bungeries; that worked out at the miserably inade-
quate rate of one bungery to every twenty, including the
infants in arms, of the population of the village. This
monstrous inadequacy has now been somewhat qualified
by the addition of a seventh bungery to this populous
village. We think that Mr. Dan Tallon ought to touch
the magic button, and that Bung should stand up as
one man and give a hip hip hurrah for the patriotic and
philanthropic magistrates who secured this pressing re-
form for the thirsty and long suffering village of Broad-
ford, in the County of Limerick. The great case came
on at the Newcastle West Quarter Sessions. In addi-
tion to that seasoned humorist, Judge Adams, the fol-
lowing graced the magisterial bench:—John French,
R.M.; Major Kelly, Dr. Magner, Captain Curling, P.
O’Shaughnessy, M. G. Barry, Eyre Tevers, Creagh Hart-
nell, and P. Vaughan. One James Sullivan was the
budding bung who clamoured at the door of Justice in
the cause of adequate refreshment for the thirsty village
of Broadford. The D.I. opposed. Judge Adams’ opi-
nion was that there was no case for the granting of the
new licence. However, Sullivan secured his bungery.
Judge Adams said :— The licence has been granted by
a large majority. It brings back the good old days. It
brings back the good old days of carefully whipped-up
benches.””  We certainly think that on this occasion
Mr. Dan Tallon should undoubtedly touch the Magic
Button, and Bung, like Jack in the box, should jump up

as one man.

We have from time to time received various articles

sent to us for our opinion. As we modestly make no
claim to be authorities on everything under the sun,
we have frequently transferred many of them to others
with a better right to pronounce upon their merits.
In the matter of razors, however, we presume we are
as sensitive as most people who shave themselves, and
we therefore have no hesitation in giving our own
opinion of a razor which we have recently had an op-
portunity of using. The razor to which we refer is
of English manufacture, being made by Messrs. T. W.
Drabble and Co., Charleston Works, Sheffield. Well,
we have no quarrel with them over that, for so long as
Irish people will shave, and razors are not made in Ire-
land, they must do with imported ones. We have found
Messrs. Drabbles’ razor (the Irish favourite), satisfac-
tory, and think it good value for 3s. 6d., the price a&
which it is retailed.

In our issue of December 20th we commented on a
rumour that the Royal Dublin Society were contem-
plating handing over the auction of horses that takes
place each Horse Show week to Tattersalls of London.
‘We now hear that the Royal Dublin Society—one of
the most bigoted corporations in Ireland so far as the
staffing of their paid posts are concerned—have aban-
doned this littla projected importation job, and the
services of the Irish firm of auctioneers are to be
retained.

Last week we published one or two instances of the
attitude of some Irish consumers with regard to the
tobacco war. We have since been furnished with an
instance from another point of view. A smoker of
Irish tobacco asked at a shop for some Irish roll, and
was handed a British-made article. He asked who was
the maker, and was given the name of the firm. “ That
is not an Irish firm,”” said he. “ Oh, if not,”’ said the
tobacconist, “at any rate, the manager of it is an
Irishman.”” ‘I didn’t come here to buy the mana-
ger,’’ retorted the customer, “but I want to buy Irish
roll,” and, of course, he got it, then. Now, what safe-
guard has the smoker of Irish roll, or, as it is popu-
larly called, “twist’’ tobacco, against having foreign
tobacco foisted on him? Evidently it is not enough
where some tobacconists are concerned to ask for Irisk
roll in view of the fact that a British-made article
stalks the market under that misleading title.

Supporters of Irish industry who want to buy the
real Irish roll or twist, and not the British-made pro-
duct of that name, would therefore do well to ask the
maker’s name when purchasing. Should they still
have any doubts, these could be set at rest in the
following way :—The brand of the Irish manufacturer
appears at intervals along the roll stocked by the re-
tailer, and by asking to see this brand every smoker of
Irish roll could assure himself that he was getting the
veal thing. If this course were generally adopted, we

‘We are hearing a lot now about future sympathetic
TIrish administration. Well, if we get it, we hope it
will include fair play for Irish industries in its scope.
Is there any chance of the Department being coaxed or
coerced into doing something to prevent the continu-
ance of this Irish-roll-made-in-Britain scandal?  The
new. Lord Lieutenant and his wife have been much
‘associated with Irish industry schemes. Is it too
much to expect that Lord Aberdeen, who will be Pre-
sident of the Department of which Sir Horace Plunkett,
is Vice-President, will cause something to be done in
the matter.

B T,

A MUZZLE FOR THE SOUTH DUBLIN UNIONISTS.

- South Dubliners who pound the drum
And Orango flags unroll,
From Bristol here to you I come
The sweepings of the poll.
(Cheers, and cries of “ You’re welcome.’’)

The Union as you are aware
Mere Irish life out pumps.
It packs off natives everywhere,
But welcomes in the dumps.
(Cheers for the dumps.)

I am a dump, not shoddy new,
‘But British second-hand ;
And so I’m good enough for you
As you may understand.
(Cries of “Too good.”’)

“Too good,” in fact I meant to say,
Though Bristol off me threw ;

And proud you ought to be this day
To have me notice you.

- (Enthusiasm accompanied with singing “ For He's a
Jolly Good Fellow.””)

No nation to my fame is dark,
It dazzles with such light.
I muzzled dogs, and made their bark
More fearful than their bite.
(Sensation.)

Then practised at the muzzle game,
That instrument to don,
To Ireland over here I came
To try the muzzle on.
(Cheers for the muzzle.)

But when I came I do declare
My job was very light.

I found that best of muzzles there
The Union fitted tight.

(Tremendous applause accompanied with the chorus
““ Glory, glory to the Union.")

But still it was a bit too free
For anti-Irish use.
The law and order thongs to me
Appeared a little loose.
(Cries of “Miles too loose.’’)

I set about to change that same,
And quickly things began

To look moxe worthy of the fame
Of Long, the muzzle-man.

Coercion, peelers, Orange flare
Choked all the native aims.
'Twas muzzle, muzzle evel:ywhere
For Ireland and her claims. "
(Cries of “More muzzle.”)
So I'm the man you should install,
The pick of British clogs ;
The muzzle-man who'll save you all
From going to the dogs. ;
(gCriez; of “ Now we sha’n’t be long.”)

Pile on the peelers and the hacks,
Your extra taxes pay;

And take the refuse on your backs
Which Bristol swept away.

Here some Orangemen made a rush, and took Long on
their backs proudly. He was heckled with one ques-
tion, and that was— Would you be prepared to make
Ireland a Crown Colony with a Loqdonderry or a
¢ Hungry Hamilton ’ governor?’ A satisfactory answer
was given. A. M. W.

LINES TO THE ELECTORS OF SOUTH DUBLIN.

Vote for the Cause—each one who can,
Your votes for Hazleton plank down ;
Not for Coercion and the man
The electors sacked from Bristol town.

See where the brute coercion stalks,
Let’s tear away another fang,

Amid the moaning of the hawks
Who constitute the Orange gang.

Long stated that he loved to hunt
Q’er Irish meadows, fresh and fair;

Give him full fling—but at the front
To enact the leading role of hare.

And hunt him over vale and lea,
And scatter all the “ gang ' besides,

But head poor Walter towards the quay
Where the great steamship “ Argo ’’ rides.

Let Goulding flee before your ire
To where the Garrison’s arrayed ;
Let modest Percy still retire
While shares are eighty in the shade.

Then Goulding safe in Orange clan
Can wear, in spite of all abuse

“ The grand old name of ‘ gentleman,’
Nor soiled by all ignoble use."”

Except when Sexton calmly shewed
That two and two make four with him,
Then took “ Sir William *’ on his goad*
And wiped the board-room floor with him.

Should our Professor play his part
And murder Walter in a song,

Your hunting’s done—for Dowden’s art
Would slay a stronger man than Long.

Should death not instantly prevail
(If there’s the smallest doubt upon i),
Let Tyrrell (of the staff of Traill)
Complete the death blow with a sonnet.

The honour of the Irish racel
every man record his vote;
Put Hazleton in premier place,
And Long—on board the Bristol boat!
RaNDALL MCDONNELL

WANTED—AN IRISH INSURANCE CO.

WING to the collapse and absorption of
O the “ National ’ and the purchase of the so-
called “Patriotic’’ by an English company, Ireland,
that has so many prosperous banks, is without as much
as one Insuwrance Company. This state of affairs is
rather humiliating from every point of view. And un-
less the Irish commercial and financial public are content
to admit that they are a rather incompetent body the
sooner they take thought and set about establishing at
least one Insurance Company the better. On the face
of it, it seems strange that a country that has established
and that maintains such prosperous and popular banks
as the Munster and Leinster and the Hibernian—to
mention only two—is in the twentieth century without
even one Insurance Company. Ireland ought to have
three or four great Insurance Corporations. Insurance,
like banking, is in the main a matter of routine, and if
we cannot produce enough and to spare in the way of
insurance clerks there is not much sense in talking of
the future of Ireland. If we can manage our own
banks—and notwithstanding an odd Scotch importation
or so we believe Irish banks are run by natives with few
exceptions—if wo can manage our own banks we ses no
reason why the managerial ability for the Insurance
business should be lacking in the country. It appears
rather ludicrous that a country that is justly clamouring
for its right to govern itself should willingly abdicate its
right to insure itself. If we could carry on the govern-
ment of the country—as we could—it is absurd that the
commercial and financial people whose natural sphere
it is do not carry on the imsurance of the country,

A friend of ours in the cowrse of a conversation re-
cently stated it as his off-hand opinion that close on a
million pounds sterling was leaving Ireland annually
as a consequence of the fact that Treland does not insure
herself.  Insurance companies, if ably and prudently

managed, are very profitable and stable concerns.

Our readers may say—Wae know all that: everyone
admits that it is a shame that Ireland has fo trust ex-
“clusively to foreigners to insure her, but where’s the

machine ab Westminster? Well, I cannot see why the
purely local Bills should need it.  As for the general

legislation, that, perhaps, would have to be submitted '

at Westminster, but as every Bill would have already
received the assent of the Ulster Council, even the
House of Lords might find itself in an awkward posi-
tion whenever it felt like throwing out a Bill. I think
the London Parliament should have no more than an
-approving or rejecting power; it should not have any
power of making internal alterations. Else you might
have this or that clique or “interest’’ intriguing at
London to procure such alterations. Say a Licensing
Bill had been carried through the Irish Assembly and
Councils after a hard struggle, it would be too bad to
have the powerful Liquor interest in the House of
Commons (and Lords, tool) operated upon by “the
Trade ** wirepullers from Ireland with intent to get the
measure nullified. The London Parliament, though
possibly not unwilling to placate the Liquor interest
_over details, might shrink from throwing out the Bill
<n toto.

It will be seen that under this scheme the Provincial
Councils would each be a sort of Lower Chamber for
local affairs, thus introducing a bit of Devolution
within Ireland itself. By this means all purely local
legislation. would reach the Legislative Assembly in a
digested state, and the rough work of sifting and sort-
ing out the fit from the unfit done beforechand. On the
other hand, the power of veto on general Bills accorded
to these Councils would constitute each of them an
Upper Chamber for general legislation. This very fact,
however, would prevent the status of these Councils
being despised, and should induce each province to put
some of its best men into them.

Of course, it would be very easy to pick holes in this,
as in any large constructive effort, and I am aware that
it seems complex. Yet I think it fairly surmounts a
rather large problem—that of giving the Northerns
local freedom, and also an interest in and influence on
the general affairs of Ireland, while at the same time
retaining them ¢» Ireland, and retaining for Ireland
some control over them. Neither party, Northern nor
Nationalist, could have any interest in behaving un-
fairly, for each side would have in its hands that check-
mating power which is the true parent of working com-
promise. As for the composition of the Cabinet at
Dublin, even there the spirit of compromise might work
to good effect, and room could be found in it for sen-
sible men of all sides, since it would be only by a reason-
able agreement between the leading elements that the
general legislation could hope to pass the Councils. Of
course, I know that those who will endure nothing but
their own views will see no light in these proposals—
the people who want a Green Flag, and the people who
want “ Popery ”’ put down. Yet, were such a scheme
in full swing of work, I think it would do Ireland—all

“THE ELOQUENT MR. DEMPSEY” AT THE
ABBEY THEATRE.
E were sorry that the Abbey Theatre
was rather a wilderness on Saturday
night.  We think the audience was the smallest
we have seen in that, so far, ill-fated theatre.
It is not for us now to search for the causes
why this theatre has not ““caught on.”” The Abbey
Theatre in some respects reminds us of a manuscripb
magazine or of juveniles playing at shop. It isn’t like
business.  Of course the Abbey Theatre people are
against commerce, even though with a rare stroke of
unconscious humour they mark up the cheapest seats at
1s., the while the bold, bad, mouney-grabbing “ theatres
of commerce *’ let one in for sixpence. In a sense, and
perhaps the attitude was not out of keeping with the
“ Celtic note,”” they went out to the fight to fail.

However, what we have to deal with now is Mr. Wm.
Boyle’s new play, “ The Eloquent Mr. Dempsey.”” This
play is preceded by a curtain raiser called  Riders to
the Sea,”” in which a corpse figures. We made it our
business to arrive at the theatre just in time to be late
for the corpse curtain raiser.

We can recommend ‘ The Eloquent My. Dempsey
to our readers; they will enjoy it. It is a great advance
on the same author’s ‘“ Building Fund.” Looking at
the play as a comedy of Irish life, the fault we would find
with it is that Dempsey is too seif-conscious a double-
dealer. In our view the heart of the real comedy of
Anglo-Trish urban life is the self-conscious patriotism of
the humbugs and double-dealers, and not the self-con-
scious double-dealing of some patriots. You consider-
ably distort the real comedy of Irish life when you put
up the Mr. Dempseys as conscious hypocrites and double
dealers. The cunning, worldly wise Irish wobbler, con-
vincing the people that he is a pure-souled patriot, and
ever occupied, with varying degrees of success, in the
endeavour to keep himself in a state of being convinced
of the same thing, is the heart of the comedy of Anglo-
Irish public life. Possibly Mr. Boyle, in making his
play less of a comedy, has made it more droll and amus-
1ng to the average audience. The play certainly is very
entertaining and is well worth seeing.

Mr. Dempsey stands before the public as a Bung, a
patriot, and many other things. He is at once-a mem-
ber of the Anti-Emigration League and an-emigration
agent. When it is announced that the Chief Secretary
is to pay a visit to Cloghermore Mr. Dempsey is in a fix.
His “ non-political "> doctor, who is a thorough-paced
Unionist, endeavours to get his signature to an address
of welcome; others of a different complexion require his
signature to a protest against the Chief Secretary. Poor
Dempsey is in_a tight corner, as many a Dempsey
throughout the country has been on various occasions.
This situation is very well developed by Mr. Boyle.
Dempsey’s endeavours to keep out of the frying pan as
well as out of the fire are very entertaining, particularly
where he gets a brief respite from his “loyal ’’ friends
by announcing that he could not think of putting him-
self forward by signing the address of welcome before
the clergy had signed it. The situation is amusingly
developed, and eventually we find that Dempsey has
signed both the address of welcome and the protest.
Dempsey is found out and is defeated at the local elec-
tion. The last stages of the development of the play,
though as amusing as any of the others, are however
somewhat unconvincing. The eloquent Dempseys are
not, unfortunately, so easily knocked over as all that.
The play would ring truer to life if Dempsey, his incon-

sistencies and double-dealing notwithstanding, came out
triumphantly at the top of the poll.

The acting reached a good all round level. Miss
Sarah Allgood as Mrs. Dempsey pleased us better than
she ever did in any of the other plays in which we have
seen her act; the character of an urban Anglo-Trish
‘matron suits her, we think, better than the character of
peasant woman, Mr. W. G. Fay as Dempsey was in his

A BOOKLET AND A BREEZE.

Sir—My respect for Father O’Leary forbids my
entering into a heated discussion with him. As T
gather from his letter that he is angry because I men-
tioned him as a possible referce (I stated expressly, if
willing to act’’) I have no hesitation in apologising to
him for having done so. I mentioned him because, as
a prominent Munsterman, his name might be acceptable
to my critic, and that the latter could not run away
from my challenge under the plea that I wished for a
packed jury from Connacht, Ulster and Leinster.

The thought that &n Moo Vipea¢ would never have
had its present shape if the Cea¢ta Deaga had never
been published is about as reasonable as to think that
the S.P.I.L. books would never have taken their shape
if Bourke’s Easy Lessons had not been previously in
existence, or that Father O’Growney, in his Simple
Lessons, imitated the arrangement of both. It might
be as reasonably said that there was a family re-
semblance between the excellent Christian Brothers’
grammar and its predecessors written by Joyce, O’Dono-
van, Mulloy, etc.; or between the I.T.S. dictionary
and -its predecessors written by O’Reilly, O’Connell,
O’Naghton, Coneys, etc. Between books for teaching
—such as dictionaries, grammars, arithmetics, etc.—
there must inevitably be some family resemblance
though some may be better than others. "It is for those
who use them to say which is the best. On the priu-
ciple laid down in this attack upon me no English
writer should write an historical novel or play because
Scott and Shakespeare had previously written excellent
works of those kinds. Such a principle would be fatal
to progress.

It was not I that dragged the name of the Ceacca
Veaga into the discussion. I have not the least desire
to disparage these books, but I have as little desire to
“rehash ’ them. Neither their authoress, for which T
h:\.vc the greatest respect, nor myself, nor any other
TIrish person can claim to be the originators of the oral
method of teaching. That method has ‘been evolved
gradually, developed and perfected by Berlitz, Gouin
and many other famous continental educationalists. The:
most that any Irish person can claim is to have applied
it to the teaching of Irish. There are numbers of con-
tinental books arranged on those lines. All Enelish
primers (and they ave legion) which T ever saw, and out
of which millions of Irish children learned English, were
arranged, however imperfectly or unconsciously, on the
same lines. In spite of their faults they have, at any
rate, been effective in making Ireland English-speaking.

I have learned languages myself on oral lines. I
have been studying them since long before any Ceactas
Deaga were known. I have spent hours, often at dis-
advantage to myself, in the British Museum Library
looking at practically every book to be obtained there
on the teaching of French, German, Italian, etc. I do
not pretend to have done more than select what I con-
sidered the best points out of various systems, and to

apply them to the teacling of Ivish. Where, then,

. with all those foreign examples in the British Museum
Library at my disposal, is the sense of assuming that
my booklet would not have taken its present form only
for the prior publication of the Ceacta Leaga? The
only point of resemblance I see between the two books
is that neither contains English exercises. The body
of my book has no English. The Ceacta Deaga have
English in the vocabularies. The preface of the Ceacta
Deaga says that they may be used for adults. My
booklet is only intended for young children.

In his reviews in the LEapEr of Part III. of my
Handbook Father O’Leary made use of the following
words : —“ Every word and every phrase and twn of
expression which I have yet met with in the book ap-
pears to me to have been taken directly from the mouth
of the native Irish speaker. It is a most im-
portant thing for the learner to feel convinced that each
phrase, as he comes to it, is not a phrase constructed by
the ingenuity of Dr. Henry, but a phrase which Dr.
Henry has heard from the mouth of some native Irish
speaker. Well, as far as I have been able to
see, there is not a single instance in the book of such
reconstruction. The learner can feel convinced that he
has before him the very words and phrases which Dr.
Henry heard from the mouths of genuine old speakers
of Irish.”” If that were so in the case of more impor-
tant work I think Father O’Leary might reasonably
give me credit for the same good faith when he finds
fault with two phrases out of an elementary book of
52 pages. I do not presume to speak for Munster, but
I know that in Connacht it is a phonetic principle that
when two words come together, one ending and the other
beginning with a vowel, one of the vowels is frequently
elided. 'Thus in such vocatives as & Una, & ataip, ete.,
the 4 is frequently omitted. I have heard this done by
the best speakers.

I repeat again that I do not resent legitimate criti-
cism. No one is more conscious of the imperfections of
my books thap I am myself. But, as the “ Irish Pea-
sant ’’ said, an anonymous charge of plagiarism is going
““beyond the beyonds.”” If I remained silent I would

be tacitly pleading guilty.

It was not without expectations of great things that
I betook myself on Thursday night, the 18th inst., to
the Theatre Royal—the one and only in the town with
the exception of “ The Gaff,”” which is reserved for the
quality. My hopes were, however, not only realised in
the high standard of the entertainment, but the method
and punctuality left nothing to be desired. The few
minutes at intervals were well filled by the gods in the
gallery, who whistled and crowed for the amusement of
the audience. It scemed a pity to have a number of
seats vacant considering the high-class nature of the
programme, but I was glad to hear that Friday night
showed a marked improvement in this direction.

The performance opened with “&n Tincéip agur an
t-Sroéos,” and concluded with Sheumas M‘Manus’s
little play, “The Hard-hearted Man,”’ while between
the two a short concert was arranged, including such
well-known entertainers as Mr. P. J. O’Shea and Miss
Hallissy.  Both were warmly received and seemed to
have been much appreciated.

The acting of both plays was very creditable for ama-
tewrs. Miss Gilligan made an ideal little fairy and ap-
parently felt her part better than in the role of old
woman. For instance, when she had succeeded in ex-
acting the necessary promise from the poor tinker, in-
stead of brightening at the idea, she looked more hope-
less than ever. The well, too, was rather awkwardly

HOW TO BE CATHOLICS IN DUBLIN UNIVERSITY.

Scene I.—Dublin. The Council Chamber of the Castle.
A University College discovered behind the Arras.

7Y HIS, dear reader, is only a mild suggestion. It
is not a solution of the University question ; it
is but an expedient of a very temporary character. The
premises of my reasoning are these—that the Liberals
are friendly to Irish Nationalists; that they have no
special care for the feelings of Trinity College—the most
Tory constituency in the Three Kingdoms—that they
will bo ready to do anything for Irish education which
is not inconsistent with their principles, and does not
give them much trouble, and by their principles Tunder-
stand their undenominational canon, that public money
is not to be given for education if the control or use
of that education is confined to members of a parbic.ula.r
religion. The reasonableness of those principles it is
not necessary to discuss here. - Now for my modest sug-
gestion of a University for Catholics on the Hire-Pur-
chase System. (The phrase is modelled on that of
Home Rule on the Hire-Purchase System, which was
used in these columns years ago, and has since attained
a wider publicity).
The begetter of the idea is Dr. Pye, for his account of
the earlier Trish University statutes in the Independent
gave the hint. Here are their provisions: —

1662.—By the Act of Settlement it was provided that
the Lord Lieutenant, with the assent of the Pr
Council (a formality), could establish and endov
College to be called King’s College in the Univer-
sity of Dublin. This Act is still in force.

1793.—The Catholic Relief Act—the real Catholic
Emancipation Act, though most people don’t know
it—enacted that Catholics might be members of
such College. It provided, however, in effect that
if the Government were to be academic, the body
who started it should not be ezclusively Catholic.

1829.—The Catholic Emancipation Act expressly recog-
nised this section, for, while repealing another part
of it, it left standing the provisions I have men-
tioned. It may be added that the small endow-
ment (£2,000) originally provided could not prob-
ably, owing to its source, be now obtained.

Where is King’s College, then? The Catholics have
been defrauded of it. The Lord Lieutenant never
exercised the powers of the Statute. It is still a Uni-
versity College 4n petto; in gremio Lord Aberdeen.
There is, as far as I know, nothing to prevent him from
calling it into existence without any legislation at any

. moment.

Scene 2.—Another part of the same:. The Green of
St. Stephen. Another University-College
discovered. )

Let us now turn to University College, Dublin. What
is its constitution? The President, Dr. Delany, has re-
cently explained it. It is governed by the President
and a Council, who are elected by the Fellows. The
Fellows are themselves elected on the ground of their
obtaining first place in the Studentship or Junior Fel-
lowship R.U.I. (or some equivalent distinction). Their
names and qualifications were given at length in a
former article in these columns. The earlier Fellows,
Dr. Hogan, S.J.; Father H. Brown, S.J.; Dr. Siger-
son, Father T. Finlay, S.J., and Dr. Cadic, were each
elected on special grounds. They had been educated
long before the Royal University came into being. They
are all well-known men. But of the nine younger Fel-
lows, eight have been elected on the same grounds as the
Fellows of Trinity College, the only ground in which
one can have implicit trust—their achievements in open
competitive examination.

It may be remembered that in my former article, I
proved, by examination rvesults, as far as the

matter can be tested at all, that they were approxi-

JANUARY 27, -1906.

- mately of equal merit to the Fellows of Trinity College.
Now, if the principle Dr. Delany has declared to be
recognised at University College, the permanency of
Fellowships, were continued; if the Fellows continued
~ to be appointed by the authorities of University College,
as far as they have a say in the matter, upon grounds
of open competitive examination ; and if the authorities
" of University College continued to be a body—not ex-
_ clusively Catholic—appointed by the Fellows, it seems
to me such an arrangement would accord with Liberal
principles. But, after all, it will be seen that the ques-
tion of the undenominational chavacter of the govern-
ment of University College does not matter to Liberals,
as I am not dealing with any distribution of public
money.

ment columns of the Daily

Whether Dr. Delany is really right trusty, or right
well-beloved. I should also make clear that in my little
scheme, after the traditional fashion of Irish legislators,
I have not consulted any party concerned. Seriously, it
will be seen that Lord Aberdeen can turn University
College into King’s College, University of Dublin, with-
out, in any way, altering its constitution. It would
s@ill maintain its relations with the R.U.I., and con-
tinue to receive its indirect endowment from that source,
but it would give its students the prestige (unmerited
but valuable) of Dublin University degrees in addition
to R.U.I. degrees. They would not, of course, have to
attend any lectures or examinations in T.C.D. Their
own would suffice. As no new money is provided, of
course, the University question is not settled. But, on
the other hand, what objection can Nonconformists or
Liberals have to the scheme as it provides no new
money?  Surely they have no tenderness for Trinity
College. Indeed, the charm of the idea is the supreme,
and I hope impotent, indignation it would stir up in
that institution. The scheme would also reconcile two
conflicting bodies of opinion among Catholics—namely,
those who look to T.C.D., and those who look to the
R.U.L. for a settlement ; and when once this College was
seen to be working as what I believe to be really an
undenominational institution—i.c., a College academi-
cally governed, and examinationally elected—an endow-
ment would not be long in coming. Moreover, such a
scheme would much increase the prospect of private
endowment.

There would, of course, be no need for au Act of Par-
liament of any kind. The thing could be done in an
instant. The power of the Lord Lieutenant under the
Act, plus the power of the King to make statutes (.c.,
ordinances) for Dublin University, would suffice. If
anyone objects to the title, “ King’s College,”’ as being
too regal—though what about the Royal University %—
even the fiercest Hungarian constitutionalist may con-
sole himself by thinking that it'is a Stuart King who is
referred to. It might be an advisable thing to add some
of the Junior Fellows, and others likely to be Fellows
in the near future (se¢ list in former article), to the
